D. He offers him Johnny Walker green as a bribe.
E. He suggests having Dan supervise the testing.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I'm leery of labs. Afraid I might go in and stay for a thousand years while cigarette-smoking men try to figure me out.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Harry
evidence_dialogues.utterance: You don't think that I would betray you in any way?

reasoning_steps.inference: John expresses fear of being locked in a lab by men trying to figure him out.
Harry attempts to reassure him by questioning if John actually believes he would betray him.

qa.question: Who does Harry verbally spar with regarding his maturity and intelligence?
option: A. John
B. Art
C. Will
D. Edith
E. Linda

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Edith
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Damn it, Dan, it's bad enough we have to listen to Harry's idiotic jokes.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Harry
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Thank you very much, Edith. Maybe when I'm 110, I'll be as smart as you are.

reasoning_steps.inference: Edith insults Harry's jokes.
Harry responds specifically to Edith with a sarcastic comment about intelligence.

qa.question: What hypothetical age does Harry suggest he might need to reach to equal Edith's intelligence?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Harry
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Thank you very much, Edith. Maybe when I'm 110, I'll be as smart as you are.

reasoning_steps.inference: Harry clearly states a specific number in his sarcastic reply to Edith.
The number stated is 110.

qa.question: What is Art's core motivation for remaining at the gathering despite his intense skepticism?
option: A. He wants to protect Sandy from John.
B. He is determined to see the situation through out of morbid curiosity.
C. He wants to wait for Harry to finish the medical tests.
D. He hopes Dr. Gruber will diagnose John with a mental illness.
E. He wants to drink the rest of the Johnny Walker green.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: All right, I think this whole thing is just a crock! I should leave, but I'm gonna stay. You know why? 'Cause I wanna see what this is all about.

reasoning_steps.inference: Art explicitly declares the story is a 'crock'.
He states his reason for staying is solely to 'see what this is all about'.

qa.question: What immediate feeling does Art project onto John when the group cannot disprove the story?
option: A. He assumes John is deeply depressed about outliving his friends.
B. He suspects John is secretly mocking and laughing at them.
C. He believes John is terrified of being locked in a lab.
D. He thinks John is grateful for Dan's philosophical support.
E. He feels John is worried about Edith's anger.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Linda
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Here you sit- You can't break his story. All you can do is thumb your nose at it.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Is that what you're doing, John? Are you laughing at us inside?

reasoning_steps.inference: Linda points out they cannot break the story.
Art immediately reacts by asking if John is secretly laughing at them, projecting mockery onto him.

qa.question: In what sequence does Art shift his focus among the attendees?
option: A. He addresses Dan's good cop routine, calls out to Will, and then accuses John of laughing at them.
B. He accuses John of laughing at them, calls out to Will, and then addresses Dan's good cop routine.
C. He calls out to Will, addresses Dan's good cop routine, and then accuses John of laughing.
D. He warns the group about telling everybody, accuses John of laughing, and then greets Will.
E. He addresses Dan's good cop routine, accuses John of laughing, and then calls out to Will.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I see what's going on. You're playing the good cop, Dan. That's fine. Just enjoy it.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Hey, Will! Will!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Is that what you're doing, John? Are you laughing at us inside?

reasoning_steps.inference: Art first addresses Dan about being the good cop.
Shortly after, he calls out to Will who has just arrived.
Later in the conversation, he questions if John is laughing at them.

qa.question: How does Art characterize Will's role as he arrives at the house?
option: A. As a cigarette-smoking man.
B. As the good cop.
C. As Dr. Freud.
D. As a Hopi shaman.
E. As a Lilliputian.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Well, let's ask Dr. Freud, who's just arrived.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Hey, Will! Will!

reasoning_steps.inference: Art announces the arrival of 'Dr. Freud'.
He immediately follows this by calling out to Will, linking the title to him.

qa.question: What is Art's explicit evaluation of Dan's conversational role towards John?
option: A. He believes Dan is acting as John's therapist.
B. He believes Dan is acting as the bad cop to intimidate John.
C. He believes Dan is acting as the good cop.
D. He believes Dan is trying to get John fired.
E. He believes Dan and John are secretly brothers.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dan
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Easy, Edith. We're just grading his homework.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I see what's going on. You're playing the good cop, Dan. That's fine. Just enjoy it.

reasoning_steps.inference: Art observes Dan trying to calm the situation.
Art explicitly defines Dan's relationship to the interrogation as playing the 'good cop'.

qa.question: According to Art, what specific number of degrees does John possess that fails to prove he is a super-wise immortal?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I have ten degrees, including all of yours...

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Ten doctorates. That's impressive, John. Did you teach them?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Art
evidence_dialogues.utterance: So much for the myth of the super-wise, all- knowing immortal.

reasoning_steps.inference: John states he has ten degrees.
Art acknowledges the number 'ten'.
Art uses this to dismiss the myth of him being super-wise.

session_1.text: Scene 9a: Dr. Gruber

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: This is fascinating. I almost wish it were true.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Y-y-y-eah, if it was true, why are you telling us? I mean, we might leave here today, we'll go out there, tell everybody!

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: It would vanish in disbelief. A story that goes around the room. No credibility. Even if I could make you believe me, in a month, you wouldn't.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Some of you would call me a psychopath, others would be angry at a pointless joke.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Well John, Some of us are angry now.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: This- This was a bad idea. Uh, I love you all, and I do not want to put you through anything.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Then why are you doing it?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: 'Cause I wanted to say goodbye-

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: ...As yourself. I think you've done that, whoever that self is.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Easy, Edith. We're just grading his homework.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: I see what's going on. You're playing the good cop, Dan. That's fine. Just enjoy it.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: All right, I think this whole thing is just a crock! I should leave, but I'm gonna stay. You know why? 'Cause I wanna see what this is all about.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: So do I. What is this all about?

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Well, let's ask Dr. Freud, who's just arrived.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Hey, Will! Will!

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Art. Hey.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: John! I'm glad I caught you. Someone mentioned that you were leaving today-

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Called you, told you that I've lost it. Glad you're here. Things are going in unexpected directions.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Yes, so I hear.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Hi. Are you hungry?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Uh, thank you, no.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Whiskey? Johnny Walker green!

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Ohhh, yes!

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (he chaperones the party to the seating)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: You look very familiar, my dear.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Linda Murphy. I'm in your Tuesday Psych 1 class, Dr. Gruber.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Ah, well, this lesson may be something I could not have imagined.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: I regret being so...obvious about this, John, but these people are all very concerned for you.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Yes, I'm cutting out paper dinosaurs.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: I really wish I'd been here from the beginning.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Me too.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Well, let me just say something right now.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: There's absolutely no way in the whole world for John to prove this story to us, just like there's no way for us to disprove it. No matter how outrageous we think it is, no matter how highly trained some of us think we are, there's absolutely no way to disprove it. Our friend is either a caveman, a liar, or a nut. So while we're thinking about that, why don't we just go with it? I mean, hell, who knows, he might jolt us into believing him, or we might jolt him back to reality.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Believing?!

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Whose reality?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: So... You're a caveman.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Yes. Uh... Uh, I was a Cro-Magnon, I think.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: You don't know if you're a caveman or not?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: No, I'm sure about that.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: A Cro-Magnon, then. When did you first realize this?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Well, when the Cro-Magnon was first identified, when anthropology gave them a name, I had mine.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Well, please continue. I'm sure you must have more to say.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Would you like for me to lie on the couch, doctor?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (laughing, patting the couch)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: As you wish.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: As a physician, I'm curious. In this enormous lifetime you describe, have you ever been ill?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Sure, as much as anyone.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Seriously ill?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Sometimes.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Of what? Do you know?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: In prehistory, I can't tell you. Maybe pneumonia once or twice. Last few hundred years, I've gotten over typhoid, yellow fever, smallpox...I survived the black plague.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Bubonic? Oh, that's terrible.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: More so than history describes.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: And smallpox- But you're not scarred.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I don't scar.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: No, John, that is not possible.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Please, let's take John's story at face value and explore it from that perspective. If he doesn't scar, it's no stranger than the rest.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: John, would you please stop by my lab before you take off, suffer a few tests from your friendly neighborhood biologist?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I'm leery of labs. Afraid I might go in and stay for a thousand years while cigarette-smoking men try to figure me out.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: You don't think that I would betray you in any way?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Walls have ears.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Medical tests might be a way of proving what you say.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I don't wanna prove it.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: So you're telling us this, the yarn of the century, and you don't care if we believe it or not?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I guess I shouldn't have expected you to. You're not as crazy as you think I am.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Amen.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: I've always liked you.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Why, thank you, dear.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Now that's changing.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Surely you don't believe this nonsense?

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: I think we should remain courteous to someone who we've known and trusted, Edith.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Here you sit- You can't break his story. All you can do is thumb your nose at it.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Is that what you're doing, John? Are you laughing at us inside?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I wish you didn't feel that way.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: What you're saying- It offends common sense!

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: So does relativity, quantum mechanics- That's the way nature works.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: But your story doesn't fit into nature as we know it.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: But we know so little, Dan. We know so little. I mean how many of you know five geniuses in your field that you disagree with... One you would like to strangle?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Ugh, strangle them all.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Damn it, Dan, it's bad enough we have to listen to Harry's idiotic jokes.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Thank you very much, Edith. Maybe when I'm 110, I'll be as smart as you are.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: If you lived as long as John did, you still wouldn't grow up.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Come on, guys. Take it easy. How often do we get to meet someone who says he's a stone-age man?

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: (mutters)

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Well, once is enough.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Edith.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: All right. A guy with your mind- You'd have studied a great deal.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I have ten degrees, including all of yours...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Except yours, Will.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: That makes me feel a trifle Lilliputian.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: That's over the span of 170 years. I got my biology degree at Oxford in 1840, so I'm a little behind the times. The same in other areas- I can't keep up with the new stuff that comes along. No one can. Not even in their specialty.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: So much for the myth of the super-wise, all- knowing immortal.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: I see your point, John. No matter how long a man lives, he can't be in advance of his times. He can't know more than the best of the race knows, if that- I mean, when the world learned it was round, you learned it.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: It took some time. News traveled slowly before communications were fancy. There were social obstacles, preconceptions, screams from the church.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Ten doctorates. That's impressive, John. Did you teach them?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Some. You might have all done the same. Living 14,000 years didn't make me a genius. I just had time.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Time. We can't see it, we can't hear it, we can't weigh it, we can't measure it in a laboratory. It's a subjective sense of becoming what we are instead of what we were a nanosecond ago, becoming what we will be in another nanosecond.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: The Hopis see time as a landscape, existing before and behind us, and we move- We move through it, slice by slice.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Clocks measure time.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: No, they measure themselves. The objective referent of a clock is another clock.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: How very interesting. What has it got to do with John?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Oh, he- He might be a man who lives outside of time as we know it.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Yes, uh, well. People do go around armed these days.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (Points at John with hand in jacket pocket, as if holding a gun)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: If I shot you, John- You're immortal? Would you survive this?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I never said I was immortal, just old. I might die. And then you could wonder the rest of your incarcerated life what you shot.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Well, uh, may I?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (Pulls out a pipe)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Everyone sighs with relief.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Preferable to a gun.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Will, that was a bit much.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (peering into a box)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Ooh. Books. Doctorates. Yes, you have grown and changed. But there is always innate nature.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Wouldn't you be more comfortable squatting in the backyard?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Sometimes I do, will. Look up at the stars.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Wonder.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: And what did primitive man make of them?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: A great mystery. There were gods up there then.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Shamans who knew about them told us.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: They still do.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Have you ever wished it would end?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: No.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Fourteen thousand years. Injuries, illness, disasters. You've survived them all. You're a very lucky man.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Pensive pause, followed by a knock at the door.
